verses.text: Then Naomi her mother in law said unto her, My daughter, shall I not seek rest for thee, that it may be well with thee? And now is not Boaz of our kindred, with whose maidens thou wast? Behold, he winnoweth barley to night in the threshingfloor. Wash thyself therefore, and anoint thee, and put thy raiment upon thee, and get thee down to the floor: but make not thyself known unto the man, until he shall have done eating and drinking. It shall be, when he lieth down, that thou shalt mark the place where he shall lie, and thou shalt go in, and uncover his feet, and lay thee down; and he will tell thee what thou shalt do. And she said unto her, All that thou sayest unto me I will do. Guidance: 'Rest' (Hebrew menuhah) is the chapter's keyword — the safe place a husband and household give a woman in an honor-based society. Naomi proposes a bold scene at the harvest celebration. The wash, anoint, dress sequence is the bride's preparation (Ezek 16:9), echoing wedding ritual. The 'feet' (Hebrew margelot) — a euphemism — and the act of uncovering them is a humble petition for marriage protection. The plan is risky: a foreign woman lying at a sleeping man's feet at the end of a harvest party. It could go very wrong. Ruth's response is the chapter's quiet courage: 'All that thou sayest, I will do.' She trusts Naomi, and through Naomi, the LORD.

And she said unto her, All that thou sayest unto me I will do. The wash, anoint, dress sequence is the bride's preparation (Ezek 16:9), echoing wedding ritual. The 'feet' (Hebrew margelot) — a euphemism — and the act of uncovering them is a humble petition for marriage protection. The plan is risky: a foreign woman lying at a sleeping man's feet at the end of a harvest party. It could go very wrong. She went down unto the floor, and did according to all that her mother in law bade her. When Boaz had eaten and drunk, and his heart was merry, he went to lie down at the end of the heap of corn: and she came softly, and uncovered his feet, and laid her down. It came to pass at midnight, that the man was afraid, and turned himself: and, behold, a woman lay at his feet. He said, Who art thou? She answered, I am Ruth thine handmaid: spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman. Guidance: The midnight scene is meant to feel charged — a single woman, a powerful older man, the smell of barley and wine, the field full of sleeping workmen. The danger of misunderstanding is total. Ruth speaks first, and her request is shaped in legal-poetic language: 'spread thy skirt over thine handmaid.' The word for 'skirt' (kanaph) is the same word Boaz used in 2:12 — 'under whose wings (kanaph) thou art come to trust.' She is asking Boaz to be the answer to the prayer he himself prayed. 'For thou art a near kinsman' — goel. She is invoking the redemption law of Lev 25 and Deut 25, asking Boaz to fulfill it in marriage. The chapter's risk is matched by the chapter's restraint — there is no impropriety, only a brave petition wrapped in covenant language.

He said, Blessed be thou of the LORD, my daughter: for thou hast shewed more kindness in the latter end than at the beginning, inasmuch as thou followedst not young men, whether poor or rich. And now, my daughter, fear not; I will do to thee all that thou requirest: for all the city of my people doth know that thou art a virtuous woman. And now it is true that I am thy near kinsman: howbeit there is a kinsman nearer than I. Tarry this night, and it shall be in the morning, that if he will perform unto thee the part of a kinsman, well; let him do the kinsman's part: but if he will not do the part of a kinsman to thee, then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee, as the LORD liveth: lie down until the morning. Guidance: Boaz's first response is blessing, not embarrassment. 'More kindness in the latter end than at the beginning' — her first hesed was sticking with Naomi; her latter hesed is choosing an older kinsman over young men. She picked covenant over romance. Then a stunning compliment: 'all the city doth know thou art a virtuous woman' — the same Hebrew phrase used of Boaz in 2:1 ('mighty man of wealth'). The same word, hayil — they are matched in moral stature. But Boaz raises a complication: there is a nearer kinsman. He will not jump the legal line. His promise is sealed with the strongest covenant oath: 'as the LORD liveth.' She is to sleep there safely; he will handle it lawfully in the morning. A man's integrity is measured by what he will not do at midnight as much as by what he will.

She lay at his feet until the morning: and she rose up before one could know another. And he said, Let it not be known that a woman came into the floor. Also he said, Bring the vail that thou hast upon thee, and hold it. When she held it, he measured six measures of barley, and laid it on her: and she went into the city. Guidance: Ruth rises in the gray dawn. Boaz speaks to protect both reputations: 'let it not be known.' This is not concealment of sin — it is wisdom about appearance in a small town. Then six measures of barley, perhaps 60-80 pounds — a sign that Boaz takes the request seriously and that he is sending Ruth to Naomi not empty-handed. The detail recalls 1:21's 'I went out full, and the LORD hath brought me home again empty.' Tonight Ruth comes home full. The book is undoing Naomi's lament one verse at a time.

She lay at his feet until the morning: and she rose up before one could know another. And he said, Let it not be known that a woman came into the floor. Also he said, Bring the vail that thou hast upon thee, and hold it. When she held it, he measured six measures of barley, and laid it on her: and she went into the city. Guidance: Ruth rises in the gray dawn. Boaz speaks to protect both reputations: 'let it not be known.' This is not concealment of sin — it is wisdom about appearance in a small town. Then six measures of barley, perhaps 60-80 pounds — a sign that Boaz takes the request seriously and that he is sending Ruth to Naomi not empty-handed. The detail recalls 1:21's 'I went out full, and the LORD hath brought me home again empty.' Tonight Ruth comes home full. The book is undoing Naomi's lament one verse at a time.

When she came to her mother in law, she said, Who art thou, my daughter? And she told her all that the man had done to her. And she said, These six measures of barley gave he me; for he said to me, Go not empty unto thy mother in law. Guidance: Naomi's question 'who art thou?' is touching — perhaps she means 'are you still my Ruth, or are you now Boaz's woman?' Perhaps she just cannot see in the dim light. Ruth tells the whole story. The detail Boaz wanted to send: 'Go not empty unto thy mother in law.' The exact reverse of Naomi's lament in 1:21. Boaz's barley is a love-note to a grieving widow he has barely met — he names Naomi's wound and sends a gift addressed to it. Tender men notice the bigger sorrow behind the immediate request.

Then said she, Sit still, my daughter, until thou know how the matter will fall: for the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day. Guidance: Naomi's pastoral wisdom. 'Sit still, my daughter.' The verb is from the same root as 'rest' — Naomi is teaching Ruth a different kind of menuhah than the one promised by marriage. There is a rest available before the outcome is known, when you have done all you can and the man — and the LORD — has the rest of the work. 'The man will not be in rest until he have finished the thing this day.' Faith is sometimes the quiet hour after the request when the wait is harder than the action. Boaz is moving; God is moving; the widows wait. Chapter 4 will tell what they were waiting for.
